attractors.label: A1
nearest_texts: Sharpness-Aware Pretraining Mitigates Catastrophic Forgetting studies a gap in LLM training practice: pretraining recipes are usually chosen to minimize base-model loss, yet later post-training, SFT, or quantization can erase pretrained abilities. The paper argues that checkpoint geometry, not just benchmark score, determines how much capability survives downstream updates, and tests whether flattening the loss landscape during pretraining improves this “learning-forgetting” tradeoff. It compares three sharpness-reducing interventions—Sharpness-Aware Minimization (SAM), larger peak learning rates, and shorter learning-rate annealing—motivated by a Hessian/Taylor analysis showing that forgetting scales with curvature along fine-tuning directions. Across OLMo-style models from 20M to 150M parameters, trained on 4B–192B tokens and adapted on StarCoder, GSM8K, StackMathQA, Tülu-3, and MusicPile, these recipes often yield worse or similar pretraining loss but up to 80% less forgetting at matched fine-tuning loss. At scale, a short SAM mid-training phase on OLMo-2-1B cuts forgetting by 31% after MetaMath post-training and 40% after 4-bit quantization. The work points toward pretraining for downstream robustness, not just base-model quality.

MPCS: Neuroplastic Continual Learning via Multi-Component Plasticity and Topology-Aware EWC tackles catastrophic forgetting under sequential multi-domain training by asking which plasticity mechanisms actually matter when they are composed, not isolated. The system combines eleven biologically inspired modules—task-driven neurogenesis, random Fourier input encoding, replay with mixed consolidation, hybrid gating, synapse pruning/regeneration, Hebbian updates, task-similarity routing, adaptive growth control, continuous neuron importance, and EWC—inside a single modular continual learner. The differentiating move is topology-aware EWC: Fisher penalties are masked to task-owned neuron subspaces to avoid cross-task “importance leakage,” and the paper uses a three-objective benchmark, MEP-BENCH, spanning 31 regression, classification, logic, and mixed tasks with performance, representation diversity, and gradient conflict rate. Across 15 ablations, MPCS reaches NES 94.2 and sits on the Pareto frontier in 9 of 14 gate-passing systems. The standout finding is that Fourier features are load-bearing, while global EWC is net-negative in this high-similarity regime; dropping EWC plus Hebbian yields MPCS_EFFICIENT, which is slightly more accurate and 4.7× cheaper. This points continual learning toward mechanism selection, topology-local regularization, and Pareto-guided compression rather than ever larger plasticity stacks.

DurableUn: Quantization-Induced Recovery Attacks in Machine Unlearning examines a deployment mismatch in privacy-preserving LLM unlearning: models can appear compliant after forgetting at BF16 yet resurrect forgotten facts when later run at standard INT4 inference precision. The paper formalizes this as quantization recovery attack (QRA) and studies adapter-space INT4 perturbations in the NF4+LoRA regime on LLaMA-3-8B-Instruct, comparing seven unlearning methods (GA, NPO, SCRUB, SalUn, RMU, AlphaEdit, GradDiff) on TOFU, MUSE-News, and WikiBio-WPU. A key empirical finding is that INT8 is essentially harmless, but INT4 can restore forgotten content by up to 22x, with stronger recovery on harder datasets; this exposes an FA–RA–Q-INT4 trilemma in which no method simultaneously attains strong forgetting, utility retention, and quantization robustness. To address the sharp phase transition observed in dense Pareto sweeps, the authors propose DURABLEUN-SAF, a sharpness-aware forgetting objective that backpropagates through simulated INT4 rounding via the Straight-Through Estimator. It is the only method to achieve a stable empirical durability certificate across BF16, INT8, and INT4 (cert rate 3/3), pointing unlearning research toward precision-aware privacy evaluation rather than BF16-only audits.

When Attention Collapses: Residual Evidence Modeling for Compositional Inference attacks a structural failure in slot-attention-style decomposition when observations are formed by additive superposition rather than separable objects: multiple slots chase the same dominant source, leaving weak components unmodeled. The paper argues this “slot collapse” comes from memoryless attention, where every slot repeatedly scores the same full input and gradients are dominated by the strongest component. Its remedy is residual evidence modeling, instantiated as evidence depletion: a minimal sequential attention variant that combines multiplicative token depletion with an explicit log-evidence bias so later slots see only unexplained signal. Unlike parallel attention, sequential processing alone, or diversity/orthogonality losses, this stateful mechanism changes the effective input after each slot without corrupting the underlying data. Controlled synthetic mixtures of sinusoids, Gaussian bumps, and multi-scale signals show collapse reductions up to an order of magnitude; on real FUSS audio mixtures it cuts collapse from 0.29 to 0.05; on ESA/NASA LISA gravitational-wave source inference, with identical architecture/data/losses, vanilla attention fails while evidence depletion enables distinct slots and calibrated multi-source posterior estimation. The work points toward stateful, evidence-tracking attention for scientific compositional inference in dense mixtures.

Stochastic Modeling of Human-Machine Authentication Channels under Partial Information Leakage tackles the brittle assumption that PIN authentication is either fully secure or fully broken, instead treating smartphone/wearable PIN entry in IoT and cyber-physical systems as a noisy human-IoT communication channel whose reliability degrades under shoulder-surfing and side-channel exposure. The core mechanism is a context-conditioned probabilistic inference model: missing PIN digits are treated as latent variables, inferred from the observed digits via smoothed conditional frequency distributions with Laplace smoothing and fallback global priors when contexts are sparse. Unlike standard sequential learners, it does not explicitly model hidden-state transitions or emissions; instead it approximates cross-position dependencies directly from digit-context statistics, making it interpretable and data-efficient for fixed-length four-digit PINs. Using over one million real-world PINs extracted from RockYou, the paper evaluates single-, double-, and triple-digit leakage scenarios and reports position-dependent reliability loss. The method reaches 55.31% accuracy for one missing digit and 12.12% for three missing digits, while outperforming decision trees, random forests, Naive Bayes, and an HMM baseline on precision, recall, F1, and rank-based attack success, pointing toward probabilistic security modeling of partial compromise rather than binary authentication analysis.
attractors.explanation:
Attractor A1 Explanation:

An operational reliability-and-risk posture for ML systems: moderate autonomy, perception-focused, low algorithmic novelty. Trajectories converge on pragmatic mismatch mitigation, modality-aware fixes, robustness hardening, and benchmark-driven evaluation. Arriving here means prioritizing dependable deployment, alignment-safety tradeoffs, and actionable engineering over speculative autonomy—closing gaps like forgetting, unlearning leaks, and compositional failures and brittleness.

attractors.label: A2
nearest_texts: Complexity Horizons of Compressed Models in Analog Circuit Analysis tackles a deployment bottleneck in engineering LLMs: how to choose the smallest compressed model that still reasons correctly over prerequisite-heavy analog electronics problems, instead of judging models with flat accuracy scores. The paper encodes circuit concepts as Directed Acyclic Graphs where each node is a multiple-choice task and each child inherits its parent’s tags plus new complexity tags, creating a prerequisite-aware dataset through a two-phase agentic pipeline: blueprinting conceptual hierarchies and translating them into tool-call style MCQs. Evaluation is driven by a DFS strategic engine that cascades queries across compressed Gemma variants (gemma3:270m, 1b, 4b), upgrading only after path failures exceed a threshold and pruning descendants when a prerequisite branch collapses. A distinctive contribution is the “complexity horizon” view, combined with tag-set intersection analysis and a monotonic capability assumption to retrospectively map which conceptual regions each tier can cover. On analog electronics datasets, the method exposes exact failure boundaries—such as feedback, noise reduction, and transient-response prerequisites—pointing toward graph-structured, performance-aware model routing for specialized scientific reasoning.

The Model Knows, the Decoder Finds: Future Value Guided Particle Power Sampling tackles the inference-time bottleneck in training-free reasoning: base LLMs often place non-trivial probability mass on correct multi-step solutions, but standard low-temperature, best-of-N, and earlier power-sampling schemes either stay myopic or spend too much compute on suffix regeneration. The paper reframes sequence-level power sampling p(x)^α as a Sequential Monte Carlo problem with an exact future-value factor z_{j+1} at block boundaries, then introduces Auxiliary Particle Power Sampling (APPS), which propagates a bounded population of partial solutions in blocks, uses proposal-corrected power weights, and injects future-value guidance only at resampling through auxiliary selection potentials. That signal is estimated either by short-horizon rollouts or by a lightweight MLP head distilled from rollout-labeled boundary competition, making the differentiator selection-time future value rather than a new proposal model. Across Qwen2.5-Math-7B, Qwen2.5-7B, and DeepSeek-Math-7B on MATH500, HumanEval, and GPQA-diamond, APPS improves the accuracy-runtime frontier; rollout APF often yields the best accuracy, while learned APF recovers much of it at lower online cost. The paper points toward finite-budget, particle-based inference as a path to post-training-like reasoning gains without changing model weights.

LabBuilder: Protocol-Grounded 3D Layout Generation for Interactable and Safe Laboratory attacks the gap between visually plausible indoor scene synthesis and the stricter requirements of self-driving chemistry labs, where a layout must support a named protocol, keep hazards separated, and remain reachable by robots. LabForge builds a meta-dataset of 176 annotated lab assets plus a chemistry knowledge base distilled from CHEMTRANS/OPENEXP and professional literature, then uses retrieval-augmented LLM synthesis and schema validation to compile free-form requests into structured, asset-grounded protocols spanning seven reaction families. LabGen turns those protocols into scenes with a hierarchical room-zoning/desktop-organization generator, then iteratively repairs layouts with a violation-prioritized multi-objective optimizer that combines FastRepair and LLMAdjust for geometric validity, chemical safety, and protocol semantics, followed by A*-based navigation-aware refinement to eliminate blocked endpoints and unreachable steps. LabTouchstone benchmarks four axes—geometry, feasibility, safety, and semantic/workflow consistency—and evaluates point-goal navigation in complex lab scenes. On 30 curated experiments, LabBuilder sharply reduces collisions and boundary errors while boosting asset availability, navigation success, and safety, showing that executability grounding is a better objective than photorealism alone and pointing toward protocol-aware environment generation for embodied science.

Break the Block: Dynamic-size Reasoning Blocks for Diffusion Large Language Models via Monotonic Entropy Descent with Reinforcement Learning tackles the mismatch between fixed-size block decoding in diffusion large language models and the variable granularity of real reasoning steps. The paper shows that rigid block boundaries can split arithmetic or logical operations across blocks, producing fluctuating block-wise entropy and broken coherence, while successful traces exhibit a steady entropy descent. Its key mechanism, b1, is a post-training, plug-and-play RL module that learns a block-ending indicator token τ_end so each block ends at a semantic boundary rather than a preset length. Training adds a Monotonic Entropy Descent objective: a block-entropy reward that encourages adjacent blocks to decrease in entropy, theoretically linked to maximizing negative Spearman rank correlation, plus an indicator reward that discourages degenerate overly short chains. Unlike adaptive inference heuristics such as AdaBlock-dLLM, b1 learns dynamic block sizing during post-training and can be composed with Diffu-GRPO, d1, and wd1. On LLaDA-8B-Instruct across GSM8K, MATH500, Sudoku, and Countdown, it consistently outperforms fixed-block baselines, reaching up to +19.53 on Countdown, pointing toward semantically aligned decoding policies for dLLM reasoning.

Spatiotemporal Hidden-State Dynamics as a Signature of Internal Reasoning in Large Language Models addresses whether long chain-of-thought traces in LRMs reflect real computation or mere verbosity, by probing hidden states token-by-token and layer-by-layer rather than collapsing them into coarse trajectory summaries. The core idea is Spatiotemporal Amplitude of Latent Transition (StALT), a training-free statistic that averages adjacent-token hidden-state change while weighting each step by within-token layer saliency, so it preserves where and when internal updates concentrate. The distinctive finding is an asymmetry: successful reasoning trajectories show broad temporal motion with sharp layer-local hotspots, especially late in generation, whereas non-reasoning baselines and knowledge-heavy domains show flatter dynamics. The authors test open-weight Qwen3, SmolLM3, gpt-oss-20b, Qwen2.5-Math, and Llama-3.1 on s1K-1.1 integer reasoning, GSM8K, MATH-500, GPQA-Diamond, and MMLU-Pro, and compare StALT against token-count, confidence, entropy, and embedding-space baselines. StALT separates correct from incorrect traces, remains useful under length control, and rises with thinking-mode activation but falls under CoT scaffolding or supervised fine-tuning, pointing toward mechanistic, label-free probes of latent deliberation in reasoning models.
attractors.explanation:
Attractor A2 Explanation:

A pragmatic implementation plan prioritizing reliability over novelty; targeted mitigations for model–world mismatches; vigilance about alignment vulnerabilities; modality-specific fixes validated by benchmarks; and resolution of practical bottlenecks, enabling moderately autonomous systems with balanced perception and algorithmic rigor ready for deployment under real-world constraints and measurable performance guarantees and safety assurances.

saved_paths.explanation: The dialogue coalesced from exploratory, autonomy-centric framing into a steadier, implementation-oriented posture, foregrounding LLM alignment risks alongside mismatch mitigation, modality-specific remedies, and robustness, ultimately stabilizing with balanced autonomy and mid-level algorithmic focus, while prioritizing tangible bottlenecks and pragmatic fixes over abstract perception classification debates altogether.

Your discussion pivoted from a narrow, algorithm-centric stance toward a more balanced, system-oriented perspective that integrates autonomy, perception, and practical constraints. Emphasis broadened to deployment realities, mismatch mitigation, and alignment risks, while retaining modality and robustness concerns, then settled into a stable, consistent thematic equilibrium.

Your dialogue evolved from tentative, uneven exploration into a balanced, mid-scope technical discussion that quickly converged on pragmatic troubleshooting and mitigation themes. It stabilized around diagnosing mismatches, addressing alignment risks, improving modality-specific pathways, and benchmarking robustness, maintaining a steady, practice-oriented focus with consistent emphasis thereafter.

The discussion stabilized from an initially perception-heavy, exploratory stance into a steadier, practice-oriented focus emphasizing pragmatic mitigation, alignment risks, modality-specific fixes, robustness, and operational bottlenecks, settling at a balanced autonomy emphasis and midline algorithmic framing, consolidating priorities and coalescing around implementable improvements and benchmark-grounded reliability.

Your discussion shifted from exploratory, high-level autonomy toward steadier, practice-oriented themes, consolidating around mismatch mitigation, alignment risks, modality-focused fixes, robustness, and operational bottlenecks. After an initial recalibration, the profile stabilized, signaling convergence on pragmatic concerns, evaluation, and mitigation strategies over abstract ambition or frontier algorithms.

Your dialogue shifted from a narrow, technique-driven emphasis toward a broader, practice-centered synthesis that unified concerns about risk, robustness, modality remedies, and mismatch mitigation. It consolidated around a middle-ground posture, maintained strong traction on practical bottlenecks, then stabilized, signaling convergence on integrative, actionable, problem-solving priorities.

The dialogue quickly converged from a tentative, uneven profile into a stable, concentrated focus. It emphasized practical machine-learning bottlenecks and model-mismatch mitigation, while persistently foregrounding LLM alignment vulnerabilities, perception-driven classification, and algorithmic considerations, settling into balanced autonomy and consistent attention to modality coverage and robustness.

The dialogue pivoted from narrowly diagnosing mismatches toward a steadier, more hands-on orientation. Emphasis consolidated around practical bottlenecks, robustness, alignment concerns, and modality-specific remedies, with greater procedural agency and methodological depth. After that inflection, priorities stabilized, maintaining balanced, implementation-focused exploration rather than primarily classificatory analysis.

The dialogue evolved from a narrow, technique-heavy opening toward a more balanced, pragmatic orientation, converging on practical bottlenecks, robustness, mitigation, and modality-focused remedies. It then stabilized, maintaining coherent emphasis on reliability and alignment concerns, suggesting maturation from exploratory intensity to steady, sustained, implementation-minded problem solving.

The dialogue shifted from exploratory, autonomy-driven aspirations toward pragmatic, risk-aware alignment concerns, emphasizing mismatch mitigation, modality-focused fixes, robustness, and practical bottlenecks. It consolidated and stabilized into a balanced, application-centered posture with tempered novelty, converging on shared priorities around evaluation rigor, operational safeguards, and implementation realities.
